question: In what year did the Baptism of Rus' take place?
fact: The Baptism of Rus' is associated with the name of Prince Vladimir Sviatoslavich.

question: Which Tsar 'cut a window to Europe' and founded St. Petersburg?
fact: The city was founded in 1703 on lands reclaimed from Sweden.

question: What event occurred on April 12, 1961?
options: End of the Great Patriotic War
Start of the Baikal-Amur Mainline construction
fact: Yuri Gagarin became the first human to journey into orbit.

question: What was the name of the first Tsar from the Romanov dynasty?
fact: Mikhail Romanov was elected to the throne by the Zemsky Sobor in 1613.

question: Under which Empress did Crimea first become part of the Russian Empire?
fact: This happened in 1783 after a series of victories over the Ottoman Empire.

question: What was the name of the Russian state legal code adopted in 1649 that definitively enserfed the peasants?
fact: The Sobornoye Ulozheniye of Alexei Mikhailovich established an indefinite search for runaway peasants, which legally formalized serfdom.

question: Who was the de facto ruler of the country during the Time of Troubles (1610–1612), earning the nickname 'Tushino Thief'?
fact: False Dmitry II set up his camp in the village of Tushino near Moscow, attempting to seize power while Vasily Shuisky ruled in the Kremlin.

question: Which state body, created by Peter I in 1711, became the supreme institution of state power and legislation, replacing the Boyar Duma?
fact: The Governing Senate was created as a temporary body during the Tsar's frequent absences but quickly became a permanent supreme authority.

question: What estate right was granted to the nobility by the Manifesto on the Liberty of the Nobility in 1762 and confirmed by Catherine II's Charter to the Nobility?
options: The right to own serf souls
The right to form noble assemblies
Exemption from compulsory military and civil service
The right to distill vodka tax-free
fact: The Manifesto of Peter III (1762) freed the nobility from 25 years of compulsory service, transforming them from a service class into a privileged estate.

question: Which Russian Emperor was killed in a terrorist act on March 1, 1881, on the embankment of the Catherine Canal?
fact: Alexander II, known as the 'Liberator' (for abolishing serfdom), was killed by a bomb thrown by the Narodnaya Volya member Ignaty Grinevitsky.

question: What was the name of the Bolshevik policy during the Civil War (1918–1921) characterized by the nationalization of industry, food requisitioning (prodrazverstka), and the prohibition of private trade?
fact: War Communism was introduced to mobilize all resources for the front; prodrazverstka caused mass peasant uprisings (e.g., Tambov Rebellion), which led to the transition to the NEP.

question: Who was the commander-in-chief of the Russian army during the Patriotic War of 1812 before the appointment of Mikhail Kutuzov?
fact: Barclay de Tolly commanded the 1st Western Army and was de facto commander-in-chief, conducting a retreat tactic that caused discontent in society and court circles.

question: In which century did the final annexation of Siberia to Russia occur, marked by the signing of the Treaty of Nerchinsk with China?
fact: The Treaty of Nerchinsk of 1689 (17th century) was the first Russo-Chinese treaty establishing a border in the Amur region, although Russia temporarily lost access to the Sea of Okhotsk.

question: Which Soviet designer led the development of the world's first atomic bomb (the RDS-1 project)?
fact: Igor Kurchatov was the scientific director of the Soviet atomic project from 1942; the RDS-1 test was successfully conducted on August 29, 1949, at the Semipalatinsk Test Site.

question: What was the name given to the standoff on the Ugra River in 1480, which marked the final end of the Horde's yoke?
fact: The 'Great Stand on the Ugra River' ended the Mongol-Tatar yoke. The troops of Ivan III and Khan Akhmat did not engage in active combat, and the Horde forces withdrew without a fight.

question: Who was the favorite and de facto ruler of Russia during the last years of Catherine II's life?
fact: Platon Zubov became the favorite in 1789 and remained so until the Empress's death in 1796; he received enormous power, which caused discontent among the high aristocracy.

question: What event took place on February 4, 1945 (February 3–11), on the territory of the USSR and determined the post-war world order?
options: Signing of the Act of Unconditional Surrender of Germany
fact: The Yalta Conference at the Livadia Palace determined the division of Germany into occupation zones, the USSR's entry into the war against Japan, and the creation of the UN.

question: What was the name of the first secular higher educational institution in Russia, opened in 1687 on the initiative of Simeon Polotsky?
fact: The Academy became a forge of personnel for science and public service; Mikhail Lomonosov and many figures of the 18th century studied there.

question: Under which ruler was the monetary reform carried out, resulting in the introduction of paper money (assignats) into circulation in Russia for the first time?
fact: Assignats were introduced in 1769 by Catherine II to cover military expenses (Russo-Turkish War) and finance monopolies.

question: Which of the following architects is not the author of building projects in St. Petersburg?
fact: Matvey Kazakov was the chief architect of Moscow (Senate building in the Kremlin, Petrovsky Palace, Golitsyn Hospital); Rastrelli, Trezzini, and Rossi worked primarily in St. Petersburg.

question: What nickname did Emperor Alexander III receive for his foreign policy line of renouncing war and maintaining peace in Europe?
fact: Alexander III, who ruled from 1881 to 1894, did not participate in any wars, for which he received the official nickname 'Peacemaker'.

question: Who was the organizer and first leader of the 'League of Struggle for the Emancipation of the Working Class' (1895) in St. Petersburg?
fact: Vladimir Ulyanov (Lenin) created the 'League of Struggle' together with Martov; this was the first attempt to unite Marxist circles for connection with the mass labor movement.

question: Which battle of the Great Patriotic War is considered a turning point, after which the strategic initiative definitively passed to the Red Army?
options: Lifting of the Siege of Leningrad
fact: The victory at the Battle of Kursk (July-August 1943) after the Battle of Stalingrad definitively consolidated the turning point in the war; the German army lost the ability to conduct major offensive operations.

question: What was the name of the elected body of local self-government introduced during the Great Reforms of Alexander II in 1864?
fact: Zemstvos (provincial and district) were introduced to manage economic affairs: road construction, medicine, public education, although the elections were curial and limited.

question: Who was the last holy patriarch to head the Russian Orthodox Church before the abolition of the patriarchate by Peter I?
fact: Adrian died in 1700. Peter I forbade the election of a new patriarch, and in 1721 established the Most Holy Synod (a collegial body) headed by the Ober-Procurator.

question: What was the name of the first Russian legal code, compiled under Yaroslav the Wise in the 11th century?
fact: Russkaya Pravda (Russian Justice) is the most important source of law for Old Rus', regulating blood feuds, fines (vira), and social relations.

question: Which operation was the code name for the German invasion of the Soviet Union in 1941?
fact: Operation Barbarossa began on June 22, 1941, opening the Eastern Front of World War II. It was named after the Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa.

question: Which Russian poet and writer was fatally wounded in a duel with Georges d'Anthès in 1837?
fact: Alexander Pushkin, considered the founder of modern Russian literature, died two days after being shot in the stomach during a duel over rumors involving his wife Natalya Goncharova.

question: What was the name of the political policy of openness and transparency introduced by Mikhail Gorbachev in the 1980s?
fact: Glasnost (openness) allowed for public debate and criticism of the Soviet past, significantly weakening the Communist Party's control over information and accelerating the USSR's collapse.

question: The Decembrist Revolt took place on December 14, 1825, in which square of St. Petersburg?
fact: About 3,000 officers and soldiers refused to swear allegiance to Nicholas I on Senate Square, demanding a constitution. The revolt was crushed by artillery fire.

question: Which city withstood a siege lasting 872 days during the Great Patriotic War?
fact: The Siege of Leningrad (now St. Petersburg) lasted from September 1941 to January 1944, causing an estimated 1.5 million deaths, mostly from starvation.

question: Who was the leader of the peasant uprising of 1670–1671 that threatened Moscow before being executed by quartering?
fact: Stepan Razin was a Don Cossack who led a major uprising against the bureaucracy and nobility. He was eventually captured and executed in Moscow.

question: What was the name of the famous 'elder' and mystic who had immense influence over the family of the last Tsar, Nicholas II?
fact: Grigori Rasputin's ability to alleviate the hemophilia symptoms of Tsarevich Alexei gave him significant political sway, which discredited the monarchy before the 1917 Revolution.

question: Which Soviet cosmonaut conducted the first spacewalk in history on March 18, 1965?
fact: Alexei Leonov spent 12 minutes outside the Voskhod 2 spacecraft. His spacesuit ballooned in the vacuum of space, making it nearly impossible for him to re-enter the airlock.

question: What was the primary cause of the Russo-Japanese War of 1904–1905?
options: Imperial rivalry over Manchuria and Korea
Dispute over Sakhalin Island fishing rights
fact: The war was fought over rival imperial ambitions in Manchuria and Korea. Russia's defeat, including the loss of the Baltic Fleet at Tsushima, triggered the 1905 Revolution.

question: The construction of which massive industrial complex during Stalin's first Five-Year Plan became a symbol of socialist industrialization?
fact: Magnitogorsk was built from scratch near a rich iron ore mountain. By 1941, it was producing a significant portion of Soviet steel, vital for tank production during the war.

question: Who wrote the 'History of the Russian State', a monumental 12-volume work completed in the early 19th century?
fact: Nikolai Karamzin's work, though criticized for its literary style and monarchist bias, sparked immense public interest in Russian national history.

question: Which battle in 1709 marked the decisive Russian victory over Sweden in the Great Northern War, effectively ending Sweden's status as a great power?
fact: The Battle of Poltava saw Peter I's army crush the forces of Charles XII. Charles XII fled to the Ottoman Empire, and the victory allowed Russia to secure its Baltic coastline.

question: What was the name of the first Russian submarine-launched ballistic missile that caused a major diplomatic crisis in 1962?
fact: The deployment of Soviet missiles (and submarines capable of launching them) to Cuba triggered the Cuban Missile Crisis, the closest the Cold War came to escalating into nuclear war.

question: Which agreement, signed in 1939, contained secret protocols dividing Eastern Europe into German and Soviet spheres of influence?
fact: The Non-Aggression Pact between Nazi Germany and the USSR included secret clauses allocating the Baltic states, Poland, and Bessarabia to the Soviet sphere, leading to the joint invasion of Poland.
